confirmed.title: Use-after-free: Splash random-pool switch callback captures a raw SwitchComponent* that a concurrent MODE/FIT rebuild frees
confirmed.line: 181-209 (capture at 181-182, deref at 203; also ETIMEOUT/EBACKEND path); rebuild that frees it at 88-97 / 320
confirmed.description: In rebuildList() for mode=="random_image" (images <= cap), each row builds a SwitchComponent (owned only by the ComponentListRow inside mList) and its toggle callback captures the RAW pointer `SwitchComponent* sc {switchComp.get()}` (line 181). Both the toggle lambda (line 182) and the splash.toggle_image RESPONSE lambda (line 193) dereference `sc` — line 203 `sc->setState(mPool.count(name) > 0)` and the failure/rollback branch at line 196 `sc->setState(!on)`. The response is delivered via pageRequest(), whose alive-token guard (MadPage.cpp:67-82) only checks the PAGE is still alive — it does NOT detect that the specific switch widget was destroyed. rebuildList() (called from setSplash()->line 320 on any MODE/FIT cycle, and from clearSavedPool()->line 345) does `mList.reset()` (line 92), which releases the sole owners of every SwitchComponent in the list (ComponentList stores rows by value, each holding a shared_ptr to the switch — confirmed ComponentList.h:24/45). After that reset the captured `sc` dangles. Reachable ordering: (1) toggle a pool switch -> splash.toggle_image (id A) in flight; (2) the toggle write/processing is slow (cold SD config write) and exceeds the 4s pageRequest default deadline; (3) user moves up to the MODE/FIT cycle row and cycles -> splash.set (id B) completes quickly -> rebuildList() frees all switches; (4) poll() then synthesizes ETIMEOUT for id A (MadBackend.cpp:236-246) and invokes the toggle response callback, which calls sc->setState() on freed memory. The same dangling deref also occurs on backend death (failAllPending -> EBACKEND_DIED -> the toggle callback) if a successful splash.set rebuild happened first. FIFO request ordering protects the common fast-path (toggle response normally precedes the mode-cycle response), so this is an edge race, not a guaranteed crash — but it is genuinely reachable. Contrast: the Systems detail page (GuiMadPageSystems.cpp:282-287/326-331) avoids this by re-locating the switch by value in the live mToggles/mRaToggles vector inside the response, and never rebuilds its list; Model2 (line 93) captures a MadChipRow* that is never rebuilt. Splash is the only page that BOTH captures a raw child pointer in an async response AND rebuilds the container that owns it.
confirmed.impact: Heap use-after-free (write through a dangling SwitchComponent*) reachable in normal use of the random-splash picker if the user cycles MODE/FIT while a pool toggle is still in flight (slow write or backend death). Likely a crash or silent heap corruption.
confirmed.fix: Don't capture the raw widget pointer in the async response. Either (a) re-locate the switch at response time by `name` against the live mList rows (mirroring how setFlag() searches mToggles), bailing if not found, or (b) capture a std::weak_ptr<SwitchComponent> (store the shared_ptr in a member keyed by name) and check before deref. Option (a) matches the existing safe pattern in this codebase. The synchronous toggle lambda (line 182) deref of `sc` is fine — the switch is alive during its own input() — only the deferred response deref (lines 196, 203) needs the fix.
confirmed.evidence:
CONFIRMED heap use-after-free (write through a dangling SwitchComponent*) reachable in normal use of the random-splash picker.

OWNERSHIP/LIFETIME (proven): In GuiMadPageSplash::rebuildList() the SwitchComponent for each random_image row is owned ONLY by the ComponentListRow inside mList (std::shared_ptr<ComponentList>). At GuiMadPageSplash.cpp:90-93 rebuildList does `removeChild(mList.get()); mList.reset();`, destroying the old ComponentList, its rows, and every SwitchComponent. The toggle's response lambda captures the RAW `SwitchComponent* sc {switchComp.get()}` (line 181) and derefs it unconditionally AFTER the request returns: line 195 `sc->setState(!on)` (error/rollback) and line 203 `sc->setState(mPool.count(name) > 0)` (success). SwitchComponent::setState writes mState and calls onStateChanged() which mutates the embedded ImageComponent mImage (es-core/src/components/SwitchComponent.cpp:58-61,77-86) — a write into freed heap memory.

ALIVE-GUARD DOES NOT PROTECT: pageRequest's mAliveToken (MadPage.cpp:72-81) only expires when the PAGE is destroyed (section switch / pop) — the page is held by std::vector<std::unique_ptr<MadPage>> mPageStack (GuiMadPanel.h:96). A rebuildList() re-run keeps the page alive, so alive.expired()==false and the dangling deref proceeds.

ASYNC DELIVERY / REACHABILITY (proven): Response callbacks fire on a LATER poll() on the UI thread (GuiMadPanel::update -> MadBackend::poll, GuiMadPanel.cpp:462; dispatchMessage MadBackend.cpp:268-288), decoupled in time from the request. Backend is single-threaded FIFO (mad-backend.py:141-153). cycleOption (GuiMadPageSplash.cpp:277-296) updates MODE/FIT optimistically and does NOT rebuild — the random-image switch rows stay on screen and focusable until the response arrives. Reachable sequence: (1) user cycles MODE/FIT -> sends splash.set S; (2) before S answers, user navigates to a switch row and presses A -> SwitchComponent::input flips state and invokes mToggleCallback (SwitchComponent.cpp:33-37) -> sends splash.toggle_image T capturing sc; (3) FIFO delivers S first -> rebuildList() frees the switches; (4) T's response derefs the dangling sc. The 4000ms default timeout (MadPage.h:88) plus the ETIMEOUT/EBACKEND_DIED error paths (MadBackend.cpp:236-242, completeWithError) route through the line-195 rollback and widen the window to seconds. No input lock blocks navigation during a pending splash request (mInputLocked is only set by the capture stream, GuiMadPanel.cpp:147).

CORROBORATING: The authors guarded this EXACT hazard with a weak_ptr elsewhere — GuiMadPageBackends.cpp:506-510 ("the rollback below may fire after a rebuild()" -> std::weak_ptr<MadChipRow>). The sibling GuiMadPageSystems raw-pointer capture (GuiMadPageSystems.cpp:220-221,252-253) is safe ONLY because it derefs sc->getState() SYNCHRONOUSLY inside the toggle callback and passes the bool by value into the request; its response never touches sc. GuiMadPageSplash differs by dereferencing sc in the ASYNC response lambda, which the code comment at lines 177-180 ("the callback only fires from the component's own input()") fails to account for.

Severity high (not critical): a genuine memory-safety hole on a normal-use path, but it requires a specific timing interleaving (cycle MODE/FIT with a pool toggle in flight) on a niche page rather than a single trivial action. Fix: capture std::weak_ptr<SwitchComponent> and bail if expired, mirroring GuiMadPageBackends.

Files: /home/deck/esde-build/ES-DE/es-app/src/guis/mad/pages/GuiMadPageSplash.cpp:88-97,181-209; /home/deck/esde-build/ES-DE/es-app/src/guis/mad/MadPage.cpp:67-82; /home/deck/esde-build/ES-DE/es-app/src/guis/mad/GuiMadPanel.cpp:456-474; /home/deck/esde-build/ES-DE/es-app/src/guis/mad/MadBackend.cpp:215-288; /home/deck/esde-build/ES-DE/es-core/src/components/SwitchComponent.cpp:26-86; comparator /home/deck/esde-build/ES-DE/es-app/src/guis/mad/pages/GuiMadPageBackends.cpp:506-510.

confirmed.title: Focus-change handler dereferences mCurrentView unconditionally — null-pointer crash in the no-games state
confirmed.description: On every gamescope focus transition the new code calls ViewController::getInstance()->startViewVideos() (main.cpp:557) and ->pauseViewVideos() (main.cpp:561). Those overrides in ViewController.h are `void startViewVideos() override { mCurrentView->startViewVideos(); }` and `void pauseViewVideos() override { mCurrentView->pauseViewVideos(); }` — they dereference mCurrentView with NO null check (unlike the sibling resetViewVideosTimer() at ViewController.h:91-95 and setHelpComponentsVisibility() at :136, which both guard `if (mCurrentView != nullptr)`). mCurrentView is initialized to nullptr in the ViewController constructor (ViewController.cpp:47) and stays null in the "no game files were found" state: noGamesDialog() pushes mNoGamesMessageBox onto the Window stack (ViewController.cpp:407) and never calls goToStart(), so mCurrentView is never assigned. rescanROMDirectory() also explicitly does mCurrentView.reset() (ViewController.cpp:1674) and, if SystemData::sSystemVector is empty, returns to noGamesDialog() leaving it null (ViewController.cpp:1689-1696). The applicationLoop runs normally while the no-games GuiMsgBox is up, so the moment the user opens the Steam overlay/QAM (focus lost), esHasFocus flips false, the transition branch fires pauseViewVideos(), and mCurrentView->... is a null dereference. Upstream callers of these methods (Screensaver, MediaViewer, PDFViewer, GuiMenu) only invoke them when a view is guaranteed active; this patch is the first caller that fires them from the top of the main loop on a purely external (focus) trigger, unguarded.
confirmed.impact: Hard SIGSEGV / crash of ES-DE. Reachable on a fresh EmuDeck install with no ROMs yet, or any time all games are removed/filtered out so the no-games dialog is showing, the instant the user brings up the Steam overlay or QAM. Not the everyday path (requires the no-games state), hence high rather than critical, but it is a definite memory-safety hole introduced by the patch in normal Steam Deck usage.
confirmed.fix: Guard both calls the same way the existing siblings do: either change the call sites in main.cpp to skip when there is no current view, or (better, matching the file's own convention) add `if (mCurrentView != nullptr)` inside startViewVideos()/stopViewVideos()/pauseViewVideos()/muteViewVideos() in ViewController.h so every external caller is safe.
confirmed.evidence:
CONFIRMED. The new focus-change handler we added in main.cpp:555-562 (verified ours via `git diff base/v3.4.1..HEAD -- es-app/src/main.cpp`) calls ViewController::getInstance()->startViewVideos() / pauseViewVideos() on every gamescope focus transition. Those overrides at /home/deck/esde-build/ES-DE/es-app/src/views/ViewController.h:87 and :89 are: `void startViewVideos() override { mCurrentView->startViewVideos(); }` and `void pauseViewVideos() override { mCurrentView->pauseViewVideos(); }` — both dereference mCurrentView with NO null check, in deliberate contrast to the sibling resetViewVideosTimer() at :91-95 which DOES guard with `if (mCurrentView != nullptr)`. (The ViewController.h overrides are upstream code, but our new caller in main.cpp reaches them on a path upstream never did.)

mCurrentView is null in the no-games state, proven on two normal paths:
1. Fresh/no-ROMs startup: main.cpp:1284-1286 calls noGamesDialog() when loadSystemsStatus==NO_ROMS, and goToStart() is only called when loadSystemsStatus==LOADING_OK (main.cpp:1301-1302). ViewController.cpp:244 noGamesDialog() only pushes mNoGamesMessageBox and never assigns mCurrentView; it is initialized to nullptr at ViewController.cpp:47. Then applicationLoop() runs with mCurrentView==nullptr.
2. All games removed at runtime: ViewController.cpp rescanROMDirectory() does mCurrentView.reset() (line ~1672) then, if SystemData::sSystemVector.empty(), calls noGamesDialog(), unblocks input, and returns to the loop with mCurrentView still null.

Reachability of the transition itself: the focus block in applicationLoop is unconditional (runs every frame regardless of which GUI is on the Window stack, including the no-games message box). GamescopeFocus.h defaults mEnabled=false / mHasFocus=true. On the Steam Deck under gamescope, init() finds the GAMESCOPE_FOCUSED_APP atom and sets mEnabled=true; hasFocus() (GamescopeFocus.cpp:124-179) self-learns mMyAppId after ES-DE is foreground ~2s (the dialog IS foreground), then flips mHasFocus to false the instant the Steam overlay/QAM steals keyboard focus. That makes `esHasFocus != sHadFocus` true and runs pauseViewVideos() -> mCurrentView->pauseViewVideos() on a null pointer => SIGSEGV. Opening the QAM/overlay is one of the most common Steam Deck actions, and the no-ROMs first-run state is extremely common, so this is reachable in normal use, not just theoretically.

(Note: on non-gamescope/desktop builds hasFocus() always returns true and mEnabled stays false, so the branch never fires there — the crash is specific to the actual target platform, which strengthens rather than weakens the finding.)

Severity high (not critical): it is a reliable hard crash on a common path, not a memory-corruption/RCE hole. Cannot run an ES-DE build per task constraints; the null-ness and code path are unambiguous from static reading, hence static-confirmed (observing the actual SIGSEGV would need the device). A one-line fix mirroring resetViewVideosTimer() (add `if (mCurrentView != nullptr)` to the two overrides, or null-guard the two calls in main.cpp) resolves it.

confirmed.title: Focus-change handler dereferences ViewController::mCurrentView, which is null on the no-ROMs / invalid-systems startup path
confirmed.description: The new GamescopeFocus block runs at the top of every applicationLoop() iteration. On a focus change it calls ViewController::getInstance()->startViewVideos() / pauseViewVideos(). Both of these (ViewController.h:87,89) dereference mCurrentView UNCONDITIONALLY: `void startViewVideos() override { mCurrentView->startViewVideos(); }` / `void pauseViewVideos() override { mCurrentView->pauseViewVideos(); }` — note that the sibling resetViewVideosTimer() DOES guard with `if (mCurrentView != nullptr)`, but these two do not. In main.cpp, ViewController::goToStart(true) (which sets mCurrentView) is only called when `!loadSystemsStatus` (main.cpp:1301-1302). On the INVALID_FILE or NO_ROMS error paths (main.cpp:1282-1287) goToStart is skipped, an error GuiMsgBox is pushed, and the application STILL enters applicationLoop() with mCurrentView == nullptr for the whole session. The focus-lost branch can only fire after GamescopeFocus learns mMyAppId (GamescopeFocus.cpp:159-160 requires ~2s of foreground), and on iteration 1 hasFocus() returns true (mMyAppId==0), so there is no startup-iteration crash; but once learned (~2s on the error dialog under gamescope), opening the Steam overlay/QAM flips esHasFocus to false → pauseViewVideos() → mCurrentView->pauseViewVideos() on a null shared_ptr → crash. I confirmed reloadGamelistView() nulls mCurrentView only synchronously within one update() call (ViewController.cpp:1484/1497), so a normal install with games has no other null window.
confirmed.impact: Crash (null pointer dereference) when, on a broken/empty ES-DE install (no ROMs found or invalid es_systems.xml), the user is sitting on the error dialog in Game Mode and opens the Steam overlay/QAM after ~2 seconds. Does not affect a normal install with games (mCurrentView is always set before the loop). Uncommon path but a real, deterministic crash once reached.
confirmed.fix: Guard the two calls: either add `if (mCurrentView != nullptr)` inside ViewController::startViewVideos()/pauseViewVideos() (mirroring resetViewVideosTimer), or wrap the focus-change branch in main.cpp with a null/has-view check (e.g. only run the pause/resume if a view exists). The simplest is to make startViewVideos/pauseViewVideos null-safe like resetViewVideosTimer already is.
confirmed.evidence:
The finding is real. Verified statically end-to-end.

UNGUARDED DEREF (confirmed): /home/deck/esde-build/ES-DE/es-app/src/views/ViewController.h:87 `void startViewVideos() override { mCurrentView->startViewVideos(); }` and :89 `void pauseViewVideos() override { mCurrentView->pauseViewVideos(); }` dereference mCurrentView with NO null check, whereas the sibling resetViewVideosTimer() (:91-95) does guard `if (mCurrentView != nullptr)`. mCurrentView is a `std::shared_ptr<GuiComponent>` (ViewController.h:191) initialized to nullptr (ViewController.cpp:47).

OUR NEW CALLERS (diff base/v3.4.1..HEAD -- es-app/src/main.cpp): the new GamescopeFocus block at main.cpp:555-562 runs at the top of every applicationLoop() iteration and, on a focus change, calls `ViewController::getInstance()->startViewVideos()` (esHasFocus true) or `->pauseViewVideos()` (esHasFocus false). These are the first callers to invoke the unguarded methods while mCurrentView can legitimately be null.

mCurrentView IS NULL ON THE BROKEN-INSTALL PATH (confirmed): mCurrentView is only assigned via goToStart() (`mCurrentView = systemList`, ViewController.cpp:655). In main.cpp:1298-1302 goToStart(true) runs ONLY when `!loadSystemsStatus` (LOADING_OK==0). On INVALID_FILE(1) or NO_ROMS(2) (enum at main.cpp:93-96), goToStart is skipped; instead invalidSystemsFileDialog()/noGamesDialog() push an error GuiMsgBox and the loop is entered with mCurrentView still nullptr. preload() (ViewController.cpp:1389) never assigns mCurrentView. The error dialogs (ViewController.cpp:222,244) only push a GUI; they don't set mCurrentView.

REACHABLE FOCUS-CHANGE (confirmed via GamescopeFocus.cpp, full read): under gamescope on the Steam Deck mEnabled becomes true. hasFocus() returns true until self-learn (mMyAppId==0 -> true, line 172), which latches after ES-DE is genuinely foreground for >2s (lines 159-160). Once latched, the first time the user opens the Steam overlay/QAM, kbdDisplay no longer matches and mHasFocus flips to false -> esHasFocus != sHadFocus{true} -> pauseViewVideos() -> mCurrentView->pauseViewVideos() on a null shared_ptr -> segfault. Opening the QAM/overlay while sitting on the error dialog is a normal user action; not attacker-controlled.

SEVERITY: medium is correct. Genuine null-pointer dereference (memory-safety), reachable in normal use, but ONLY on a broken/empty install (no ROMs found or invalid es_systems.xml) — a working install always reaches goToStart and mCurrentView is non-null, so the common path is unaffected. Trivial fix matching the existing guarded sibling: null-check mCurrentView (or guard the two new calls in main.cpp).

confirmed.title: Per-frame setBlockInput(!esHasFocus) overrides ViewController's intentional input-block during game launch / rescan
confirmed.description: The gamescope block runs at the very top of every loop iteration and calls window->setBlockInput(!esHasFocus) unconditionally (main.cpp:549), before SDL_PollEvent and window->update(). ViewController also drives the same flag: triggerGameLaunch() sets setBlockInput(true) (ViewController.h:101), the launch animation runs for `duration` ms (default 3000, see ViewController.cpp:1056) before the blocking launchGame, the post-return workaround animation re-blocks then clears it (ViewController.cpp:1085-1086), and rescanROMDirectory() sets setBlockInput(true) (ViewController.cpp:1668). mBlockInput is the ONLY input gate in Window::input (Window.cpp:243-244). Because ES-DE normally still HAS gamescope focus during the launch-screen delay, esHasFocus is true, so setBlockInput(false) is reasserted every frame, clobbering the setBlockInput(true) those flows rely on. Practical impact is limited: during the launch-screen delay Window::input (Window.cpp:265-269) consumes a keypress to dismiss the launch screen rather than navigate, and the post-game return is largely covered by the new >500ms input-flush (main.cpp:524-538) since the loop is frozen during the synchronous game run. With LaunchScreenDuration=disabled there is no delay window at all. So this is mostly benign today, but it silently defeats the upstream "all user input is blocked" guarantee and is fragile: any future flow that relies on setBlockInput(true) persisting across frames while ES-DE is focused will be broken by this unconditional per-frame write.
confirmed.impact: ES-DE's intentional input-block windows (launch animation, rescan, return-from-game key swallow) are reset to unblocked every frame whenever ES-DE has focus. Today the visible effect is minor (a stray button press during the launch-screen countdown can dismiss the launch screen early), but the input-blocking contract is no longer honored.
confirmed.fix: Don't blindly assert the flag every frame. Track the focus-derived desire separately and only setBlockInput(true) on focus loss / setBlockInput(false) on focus regain (i.e. on the transition, like the video pause already does at main.cpp:555-563), instead of writing it every iteration. Better still, OR the focus state with the existing block rather than overwriting, so ViewController's blocks survive.
confirmed.evidence:
The patch adds, at the very top of every applicationLoop() iteration (main.cpp:547-549), an UNCONDITIONAL write: gamescopeFocus.init(); const bool esHasFocus {gamescopeFocus.hasFocus()}; window->setBlockInput(!esHasFocus); — this runs BEFORE SDL_PollEvent/parseEvent and BEFORE window->update(). The diff base/v3.4.1..HEAD confirms upstream had no per-frame setBlockInput at the loop top (only an Android-specific path).

mBlockInput is the ONLY input gate: Window::input() (Window.cpp:243-244) early-returns iff mBlockInput. grep confirms there is no separate launch-screen input guard.

Launch-animation conflict (the strong, confirmed case): ViewController::triggerGameLaunch (ViewController.h:98-102) sets mWindow->setBlockInput(true). The next ViewController::update (ViewController.cpp:1329-1333) calls launch()->launchGame(), which starts a LambdaAnimation of `duration` ms (default 3000, ViewController.cpp:1056,1072) whose COMPLETION callback runs the blocking game->launchGame(). setAnimation is non-blocking, so the loop iterates for the full ~3 s. Comment at ViewController.cpp:1069-1071 states the intent: "During this time period, all user input is blocked." During those 3 s ES-DE still holds gamescope focus (the game process hasn't started), so GamescopeFocus::hasFocus() returns true (GamescopeFocus.cpp:124-179; also returns true whenever !mEnabled or mMyAppId==0). Therefore line 549 writes setBlockInput(false) every frame, and the subsequent parseEvent in the same iteration delivers input to the ViewController behind the launch screen. Reachable in normal use: a button tap/dpad during the ~3 s launch screen gets processed (stray navigation, opening a menu, leaving the UI in an unexpected state on return). Not a crash/corruption/memory-safety issue -> medium, matching the finding.

Partial overstatement: the rescan claim is weak. rescanROMDirectory (ViewController.cpp:1666-1708) sets block true, then does SystemData::loadConfig()/preload() SYNCHRONOUSLY inside one update() call, then sets block false; the loop does not iterate to re-clear the flag mid-scan, so line 549 does not defeat the rescan block (aside from the single frame entering/leaving). The return-from-game re-block (ViewController.cpp:1085-1086) is a 1 ms window, negligible. The core mechanism (launch-animation block defeated) is real and confirmed.

confirmed.title: Reader thread accumulates an unbounded line buffer when the daemon emits a long line with no newline
confirmed.description: readerLoop() appends every 4096-byte read into std::string buffer and only erases up to the last '\n' it found (buffer.erase(0, pos)). There is no cap on buffer length. If the daemon ever emits a very large JSON line, or a stream of bytes containing no newline (e.g. a wedged/garbled writer, a binary blob accidentally written to stdout, or a malformed mega-response), buffer grows without bound and consumes memory on the reader thread until OOM. NDJSON framing assumes a newline-terminated line of bounded size; nothing enforces that on the C++ side. parseLine is only ever attempted on a completed line, so a never-terminated line is never even rejected.
confirmed.impact: A buggy or hostile daemon writing megabytes without a newline grows ES-DE's RSS unbounded with no error surfaced. In normal operation the daemon emits small newline-terminated lines, so this is an edge-case robustness gap, not a normal-path bug.
confirmed.fix: Cap buffer (e.g. if buffer.size() exceeds a few MB with no newline, log a warning, clear it, and treat the stream as corrupt / trigger a restart). Even a generous 1-4 MB ceiling bounds the worst case.
confirmed.evidence: MadBackend.cpp readerLoop (134-160): buffer.append(chunk,count) accumulates every 4096-byte read; buffer.erase(0,pos) only trims up to the last seen '\n'. A line with no newline grows the std::string without bound (no cap/limit). Real, but it runs on the dedicated reader thread (not the UI/render thread) and the producer is the trusted local mad-backend.py emitting NDJSON, so an unterminated multi-MB line is a daemon-bug-only scenario. Robustness only -> low.

confirmed.title: Blocking write() to the daemon's stdin pipe runs on the UI/render thread and can stall the frame if the daemon stops reading
confirmed.description: The panel->daemon pipe is created with pipe2(inPipe, O_CLOEXEC) only (no O_NONBLOCK, line 79), so mStdinFd is a blocking fd. writeLine() loops on write() and is always invoked on the UI thread (request() is only ever called from page build/input/update and poll-driven callbacks, all UI-thread). If the daemon is alive but stops draining its stdin (e.g. a slow/hung handler that doesn't return to the read loop) and ~64KB of unread requests accumulate, write() blocks the render thread until the pipe drains, freezing the whole ES-DE UI. The reader thread keeps the daemon->panel direction unblocked, but the panel->daemon direction has no such protection.
confirmed.impact: Realistically bounded: requests are small (hundreds of bytes) and only issued on discrete user actions, so reaching the ~64KB pipe-full threshold requires the daemon to be wedged not-reading while the user rapidly fires actions. A wedged daemon is normally caught by the 4s request timeout, but the timeout fires from poll() which can't run if write() in request() is blocked first. Edge case, low likelihood, but it is a UI-thread blocking I/O on a path that depends on the daemon's good behavior.
confirmed.fix: Make mStdinFd non-blocking and treat EAGAIN as backpressure (drop/queue the request or fail it with EBACKEND_DIED after a short bounded spin), or bound the write with a poll()/select() timeout so a wedged daemon can't freeze the render thread.
confirmed.evidence: writeLine (414-430) loops on blocking write(mStdinFd,...) with no O_NONBLOCK; request() is invoked from UI-thread callbacks (poll()->dispatchMessage->callback, and GuiMadPanel button handlers). If the kernel pipe buffer to the daemon's stdin fills because the daemon stopped reading, write() blocks the render thread. SIGPIPE is ignored (spawn() 62-68) so a *broken* pipe returns EPIPE cleanly; the stall is only the daemon-alive-but-not-reading case. Requests are tiny JSON lines and the default pipe buffer is 64KB, so filling it requires a wedged daemon. Edge-case robustness -> low.

confirmed.title: Panel can wedge all input if the daemon's input.lock 'locked:false' event is missed (no client-side watchdog)
confirmed.description: mInputLocked is set/cleared SOLELY by the daemon's 'input.lock' event (locked:true/false). The capture modal does not clear it on its own teardown. When mInputLocked is true and not nav-allowed, GuiMadPanel::input() does `if (mInputLocked && !mInputLockAllowNav) return true;` BEFORE any B/LB/RB handling, so the panel swallows literally every input (cannot press B to leave, cannot switch sections). The only client-side resets are onBackendReady() (after a backend death/restart) and showError(). If the daemon ever sends a capture result/{closed} but the matching 'input.lock locked:false' is dropped (the generic-event cap is FIFO-drop-oldest at 256, or a daemon protocol bug) and the backend does NOT subsequently die, the panel becomes permanently unresponsive after the capture modal pops. There is no timeout, no 'unlock if no modal is on the stack' safety net, and no key/combo escape.
confirmed.impact: User-facing soft-lock of the MAD panel requiring ES-DE to be killed. Reachability is low (depends on the daemon failing to send the unlock event while staying alive), but the failure mode is total input loss with no recovery and nothing logged.
confirmed.fix: Add a client-side safety reset: clear mInputLocked whenever the panel regains topmost focus with no GuiMadCaptureModal on the Window stack, or arm a watchdog that clears the lock if held past the daemon's capture timeout (~20s) without a result. Even a 'B held 2s force-unlock' escape hatch would prevent the unrecoverable state.
confirmed.evidence:
The mechanic is real and confirmed in the actual code, but I downgrade severity from medium to low: the backend pairs lock/unlock robustly and the only in-transit loss path is implausible in normal use.

CONFIRMED FACTS:
- GuiMadPanel.cpp:411 — `if (mInputLocked && !mInputLockAllowNav) return true;` runs BEFORE all B/LB/RB/trigger handling (lines 414-447). So when locked-without-nav, the panel swallows every non-keyboard input, including B — the user genuinely cannot press B to leave or switch sections.
- mInputLocked is cleared client-side at ONLY 3 sites: the `input.lock` event callback (GuiMadPanel.cpp:148), onBackendReady() (line 163, runs on backend death/restart), and showError() (line 192). I grepped every `mInputLocked =` assignment to confirm.
- The capture modal does NOT self-clear the lock: GuiMadCaptureModal::finish() (lines 179-198) and ~GuiMadCaptureModal() (lines 93-99) only clear the stream callback and delete the modal; on cancel (line 210) it fires `capture.cancel` fire-and-forget and relies on the daemon to emit `input.lock locked:false`.

WHY SEVERITY IS LOW (reachability is very low — the finding itself admits this):
- The backend emits the unlock robustly on EVERY exit path. The Stream framework (rpc.py:114-126) calls cleanup() in a `finally` whenever run() returns/raises for any reason; capture_cmds.py cleanup() (lines 162-171) and daphne_cmds.py (finally, lines 204-205) and tester_cmds.py (cleanup, lines 632-635/844-847 gated on self.locked) all emit `locked:false`. capture.cancel -> stop_stream -> stopped.set -> run() exits -> finally -> cleanup -> unlock (capture_cmds.py:190-194). Daemon teardown (stop_all_streams, rpc.py:147-155) and a superseding capture (capture.button stops prev, capture_cmds.py:181-186) also unlock.
- A backend DEATH is already handled (onBackendReady clears the lock on auto-restart, MadBackend.cpp auto-restart at lines 547-561).
- The ONLY way to wedge while the daemon stays alive is losing the `locked:false` event in transit. input.lock is framed as `{"event":"input.lock"}` (rpc.py:43-45), NOT `{"event":"stream"}`, so it does NOT hit the coalescing path (MadBackend.cpp:178-194). It IS subject to the queue cap (MadBackend.cpp:196-209), which drops the OLDEST event when eventCount >= MAX_QUEUED_EVENTS (256, line 25). Triggering that requires >256 events queued between two poll()s — but the capture modal polls every frame in its own update() (GuiMadCaptureModal.cpp:216-222) and the panel polls every frame when topmost, so reaching 256 events in ~16ms from human button presses + (coalesced) device-watch snapshots is not plausible in normal use.

NET: The "no client-side watchdog / auto-timeout to clear a stale lock" observation is a genuine defense-in-depth gap and the input()-swallows-B mechanic is exactly as described. But it does not corrupt data, never leaks input to the views below, is recoverable (kill ES-DE), and the in-transit-drop trigger is implausible. Low, not medium. A cheap hardening would be a client-side timestamped lock-staleness timeout or clearing mInputLocked when the capture modal pops.

confirmed.title: Backend teardown does a synchronous ~2-4s blocking reap on the UI/render thread (panel close, RETRY, crash path)
confirmed.line: 446-486 (shutdownChild), reached from GuiMadPanel.cpp:423 delete this, :129 RETRY restart(), and poll() handleChildDeath
confirmed.description: shutdownChild() sends SIGTERM then busy-waits up to 40x50ms (2s) for the child, escalating to SIGKILL + a blocking waitpid(0), then waits up to another 2s for the reader thread before joining. All of this runs on the UI thread: on panel close (B at root -> delete this -> ~GuiMadPanel -> ~MadBackend -> terminate -> shutdownChild), on RETRY (mRetryButton callback -> mBackend->restart()), and on the crash auto-restart path (poll -> handleChildDeath -> shutdownChild) which itself runs inside GuiMadPanel::update()/GuiMadCaptureModal::update(). A daemon that ignores SIGTERM plus a stuck pipe read can stall rendering/input for up to ~4 seconds with a frozen frame.
confirmed.impact: A multi-second visible UI freeze (frozen panel, no input) on close/retry/crash if the Python backend is slow or hung. Not a crash or memory-safety issue and the worst case requires a misbehaving daemon, but it blocks the render thread by design.
confirmed.fix: Acceptable for a control panel; consider shortening the SIGTERM grace (e.g. 500ms before SIGKILL on the close path), or showing a 'closing...' frame. At minimum document the deliberate stall. No code change strictly required.
confirmed.evidence: shutdownChild (446-486) reaps with up to 40 iterations of sleep_for(50ms) = ~2s before SIGKILL, then up to another 40x50ms = ~2s waiting for the reader thread to finish before join — worst case ~4s of std::this_thread::sleep_for. It is reached on the UI/render thread: GuiMadPanel::update()->mBackend->poll()->handleChildDeath()->shutdownChild (crash path), RETRY button -> mBackend->restart()->terminate()->shutdownChild (GuiMadPanel.cpp:129), and `delete this` panel close (:392/:402/:423) -> ~MadBackend->terminate()->shutdownChild. Confirmed the blocking-on-UI-thread mechanism. But the full ~2-4s only occurs when the Python daemon ignores stdin-EOF + SIGTERM; a normal daemon exits in milliseconds, so the loop returns almost immediately. Teardown/crash path only, not a steady-state hitch -> low.

confirmed.title: Unguarded rapidjson GetString() on Wii accessory "allowed" array element
confirmed.description: In GuiMadPageGamepadTest::rebuild(), the ext.allowed array is iterated with only an outer `allowedArr.IsArray()` check; each element is read directly via `allowedArr[i].GetString()` with NO per-element `IsString()` guard (unlike every other value-array loop in these four pages, e.g. GuiMadPageQuitCombo.cpp:151/167, GuiMadPagePriority.cpp:684, GuiMadPageGamepads.cpp:769, which all check IsString() per element). rapidjson's GetString() does `RAPIDJSON_ASSERT(IsString())` which is `assert()` and is compiled OUT in the release build (CMakeLists.txt:198 defines -DNDEBUG). With the assert gone, GetString() on a non-string element returns the value union's string-pointer field reinterpreted from an object/array/number, so `std::string(badPtr)` reads from an arbitrary address until a NUL.
confirmed.impact: If mad-backend.py ever emits ext.allowed containing a non-string element (e.g. a nested array, number, or null), this is a heap over-read / likely crash on the gamepad-tester Wii path. Reachability is low because the backend is our own trusted Python and currently sends a string array; this is defense-in-depth against a backend bug or protocol drift, matching the per-element guards used everywhere else.
confirmed.fix: Mirror the other loops: `for (...) { if (allowedArr[i].IsString()) extAllowed.emplace_back(allowedArr[i].GetString()); }`.
confirmed.evidence: GuiMadPageGamepads.cpp:491-492 loops `extAllowed.emplace_back(allowedArr[i].GetString())` with NO IsString() guard — unlike the project's own MadJson::getString (MadJson.h:54 checks IsString()) and unlike the parallel sections loop in GuiMadPageDaphne.cpp:116 which DOES check `if (it->value[i].IsString())`. rapidjson GetString() is `RAPIDJSON_ASSERT(IsString()); return DataString(data_)` (external/rapidjson/include/rapidjson/document.h:1853). RAPIDJSON_ASSERT resolves to plain assert() (rapidjson.h:437), and the release build defines -DNDEBUG (CMakeLists.txt:198, comment at line 194: 'will also disable all assert() macros'). So in the shipped AppImage a non-string element does NOT abort — GetString() reinterprets the value union's string-pointer field from a number/bool/object/array, returning a garbage const char* that std::string's ctor runs strlen() on -> OOB read / crash (UB). Reachability: NOT a normal path. The trusted local Python backend always emits allowed as `sorted(EXT_BTNS.get(ext, frozenset()))` (lib/madsrv/tester_cmds.py:269) — a list of strings — so this only triggers if a malformed/corrupted backend sends a non-string array element. Real defensive gap, inconsistent with the codebase's own guarded helpers, but only exploitable with a misbehaving daemon. Low is correct.

confirmed.title: std::clamp with potentially inverted bounds in Model2 number steppers (theoretical UB if backend min > max)
confirmed.description: addNumberStepper() reads lo=numberAt("min"), hi=numberAt("max") straight off the backend JSON, then does `std::clamp(cur, lo, hi)` (line 172). std::clamp has undefined behavior when hi < lo. The same inverted-bound range is then passed to addStepper(label, lo, hi, step, ...). The header comment states the backend owns a curated key set with a comment-preserving write, so min/max come from a trusted curated table rather than arbitrary user data — making a min>max payload very unlikely in practice. But there is no defensive check, so a backend bug or a tampered model2.get response could trigger UB.
confirmed.impact: Undefined behavior (in practice usually returns lo or hi) only if a malformed/buggy backend sends min>max for a Model2 numeric setting. Not reachable from trusted backend data.
confirmed.fix: Guard before clamping: `if (hi < lo) std::swap(lo, hi);` (or skip the setting), then clamp. Cheap and removes the UB edge entirely.
confirmed.evidence: GuiMadPageModel2.cpp:169-172: lo=numberAt(setting,'min',0.0), hi=numberAt(setting,'max',...), then std::clamp(value, lo, hi). std::clamp has UB when lo>hi (precondition !(hi<lo)). Bounds are backend-supplied, but the backend defines them in a hardcoded constant table where every entry has min<max (lib/madsrv/model2_cmds.py:60-71, e.g. min 0.5/max 2.5, min 0/max 9). The defaults passed when absent (0.0 / 2.5 or 9.0, line 169-170) are also min<max. So lo>hi is only possible from a malformed backend response — never in normal operation. Theoretical UB only, exactly as titled; not reachable in normal use. Low is the correct (generous) severity; arguably info given non-reachability.

confirmed.title: MadWiiBridge::spawn sets SIGPIPE to SIG_IGN process-globally and never restores it, defeating MadBackend's deliberate save/restore and changing every launched game's signal environment
confirmed.description: MadWiiBridge::spawn() is called once at startup (main.cpp:1387, before applicationLoop) and does `signal(SIGPIPE, SIG_IGN)` with no save and no restore (shutdown() never restores it). Upstream ES-DE never touches SIGPIPE (verified: no SIGPIPE/signal() in main.cpp or PlatformUtil.cpp), so by default games inherit the default terminate disposition. After this patch, the ES-DE process and every game launched via launchGameUnix (fork+exec) inherit SIGPIPE=SIG_IGN. This also undermines MadBackend.cpp's careful design: MadBackend saves the *previous* SIGPIPE disposition in spawn() (MadBackend.cpp:62-68) and restores it in ~MadBackend (lines 45-49) specifically `so launched games don't inherit it` — but because MadWiiBridge ran first and globally set SIG_IGN, the disposition MadBackend captures IS SIG_IGN, so its restore is a no-op and SIGPIPE stays ignored for the whole session regardless. The MadWiiBridge comment acknowledges children inherit it and calls it `the benign direction`.
confirmed.impact: No crash. Every launched emulator/game inherits SIGPIPE=SIG_IGN instead of the default. For the vast majority of games this is harmless (they check write() return codes), but it is a silent, process-wide signal-environment change that contradicts MadBackend's stated intent and could in principle alter behavior of a child that relies on dying on SIGPIPE. It also makes MadBackend's save/restore dead code in practice.
confirmed.fix: Have MadWiiBridge save the previous SIGPIPE disposition with sigaction(&prev) before setting SIG_IGN and restore it in shutdown() (mirroring MadBackend), or, better, set the ignore disposition once in a single shared place before either bridge/backend spawns and restore it around game launches only. At minimum, ensure MadWiiBridge runs its save/restore so MadBackend's restore is meaningful.
confirmed.evidence: MadWiiBridge::spawn (MadWiiBridge.cpp:51) calls `signal(SIGPIPE, SIG_IGN)` process-globally with no save and no restore (only after the existence guard at line 44, so it only fires when ~/Emulation/tools/launchers/wii-nav-bridge.py exists). It runs once at startup (main.cpp:1390) BEFORE MadBackend ever spawns. MadBackend deliberately saves/restores SIGPIPE (spawn 62-67 captures mPrevSigpipe; ~MadBackend 48-49 restores) specifically 'so launched games don't inherit a process-global SIG_IGN' (MadBackend.h:130-132). But because MadWiiBridge already set the global disposition to SIG_IGN first, MadBackend's spawn captures SIG_IGN as the 'previous' value and its destructor restores it to SIG_IGN -- the restore is moot. Games launched via launchGameUnix (PlatformUtil.cpp:181/193 popen/system through /bin/sh) therefore inherit SIG_IGN. The candidate's description is accurate. Impact is benign by design: MadWiiBridge.cpp:48-50 explicitly accepts it ('emulators handling EPIPE instead of dying on SIGPIPE is the benign direction'). Real behavioral inconsistency, narrow trigger, harmless direction -> low.

confirmed.title: ApplicationUpdater::compareVersions still can throw std::out_of_range from std::stoi on an all-digit but oversized release number, on an unguarded updater thread
confirmed.description: The patch adds a digit pre-check `releaseNum.find_first_not_of("0123456789") != npos` to avoid std::stoi throwing std::invalid_argument on non-numeric feed values (a genuine improvement over upstream, whose raw std::stoi could already throw). But std::stoi still throws std::out_of_range when the string is all digits yet exceeds INT_MAX (>= ~2147483648, i.e. 10+ digit run numbers). compareVersions() runs on updaterThread() and is called at line 131 WITHOUT a try/catch (only downloadFile()'s update() and parseFile() are wrapped, lines 148/174). An uncaught exception propagating out of the std::thread function calls std::terminate(). The feed is fetched over HTTPS from github.com/mmadalone/mad/releases (trusted CI artifact, not arbitrary-attacker-controlled), and GITHUB_RUN_NUMBER won't reach 10 digits for a long time, so reachability is narrow.
confirmed.impact: If the release feed ever contains an all-digit release number >= 2^31 (CI mistake, manual edit, or compromised release asset), the in-app updater thread throws std::out_of_range uncaught → std::terminate aborts ES-DE at startup. The patch already covers the common (non-numeric) case; this is the residual numeric-overflow edge.
confirmed.fix: Wrap the std::stoi in try/catch (catch std::exception, log + continue) or parse with std::strtol and validate the range, instead of relying solely on the digit-only pre-check. Catching in compareVersions is cheap and matches the pattern already used for downloadFile/parseFile.
confirmed.evidence: ApplicationUpdater.cpp:419-425: the added guard only rejects non-digit characters (find_first_not_of("0123456789")), then calls std::stoi(releaseType->releaseNum) at line 425. An all-digit but oversized string (e.g. 20 digits) passes the guard yet overflows int, so std::stoi throws std::out_of_range (well-defined C++17 [string.conversions] behavior; the patch comment at 414-415 even states the goal is that 'a malformed releaseNum must not throw on the updater thread (uncaught = abort at startup)'). compareVersions() is invoked at updaterThread() line 131 with NO try/catch wrapping it (the only try/catch, 148-155, is inside downloadFile() around update()). updaterThread runs on a std::thread (line 114), so an uncaught exception there calls std::terminate -> abort. The guard the patch added is incomplete exactly against its own stated intent. Real but practically unreachable: the feed is CI-generated and 'release' is a GitHub Actions run number (small integer), so a 10+ digit value won't occur in practice -> low.

confirmed.title: No XSetIOErrorHandler — a broken X connection to the gamescope server terminates ES-DE via Xlib's default fatal handler
confirmed.description: GamescopeFocus opens its own Display connection to the primary X server (tryDisplay, line 65-76) and polls it every frame in hasFocus() via XGetWindowProperty (readCardinal, line 37-59). readCardinal checks the XGetWindowProperty return value, but Xlib I/O errors (the X connection itself breaking — server restart, socket reset) are not returned from these calls: Xlib invokes the process-global XIOErrorHandler, whose default implementation prints a message and calls exit(1). No custom handler is installed (XSetIOErrorHandler / XSetErrorHandler are absent from the file). On the Steam Deck the primary server :0 is shared with Steam and stable for the session, so this is low-probability, but if that connection ever drops while ES-DE is polling it, the default Xlib handler would kill ES-DE rather than letting the feature fail safe (which the code otherwise tries hard to do — note the deliberate fail-toward-focused at line 145-148).
confirmed.impact: If the gamescope/primary X connection breaks mid-session, ES-DE is terminated by Xlib's default I/O error handler instead of degrading gracefully (the intended behavior is to keep running with the pause feature failing toward 'focused').
confirmed.fix: Install an XSetIOErrorHandler (and optionally XSetErrorHandler) that marks mEnabled=false and returns/longjmps instead of letting the default exit() fire, so a lost connection disables the feature rather than crashing ES-DE.
confirmed.evidence: Grep over es-app/src and es-core/src finds no XSetIOErrorHandler or XSetErrorHandler anywhere. GamescopeFocus.cpp opens a Display to the gamescope primary X server (init() 80-122, tryDisplay 65-76) and polls it every frame via hasFocus() (124-179), which is called from the main render loop (main.cpp:548). Xlib's default I/O error handler, invoked when the X connection breaks (server restart/crash, fd dies), prints a message and calls exit() -- it terminates ES-DE. The candidate is accurate: a broken connection to the gamescope :0 server would kill ES-DE rather than fall back to the existing 'fail toward focused' logic. Low severity: the primary gamescope X server restarting mid-session is uncommon on the Steam Deck, it is reliability (not memory safety), and the per-frame poll only matters while native PauseGames is enabled.

confirmed.title: romhack-art-urls.json is orphaned: no code path consumes it
confirmed.description: This 36-entry JSON list of {system, stem, url} romhack box-art URLs (systems snesh/genh/snesmsu1) has NO consumer anywhere in the launchers tree. Exhaustive grep for the basename, 'romhack-art', 'art-urls', and 'art_urls' across all .py/.sh/.md/docs files and the ES-DE fork returns only this file itself and an unrelated comment in wire-bezels.py (line 5, the word 'romhacks'). skyscraper-apply.py works off /tmp/sky-*/media, not this file; steam-fetch-media.py is Steam-only. The JSON itself is well-formed (valid list, union of keys exactly {system,stem,url}, no duplicate (system,stem) pairs, all URLs http(s)). So this is dead/orphaned data: either a manual one-off art-download manifest that is run by hand outside the repo, or the fetch/apply wiring that was supposed to consume it was never committed. 3 of the 36 URLs point at fragile hosts (web.archive.org snapshots and an img.youtube.com thumbnail) that may rot.
confirmed.impact: No runtime impact (nothing reads it, so it cannot crash or mis-render anything). The gap is maintainability/intent: a reader of the repo cannot tell whether romhack art is actually applied from this file or whether a wiring step is missing. If a fetch step was intended, romhack systems silently get no curated art.
confirmed.fix: Decide intent: (a) if a fetch/apply step exists outside the repo, add a one-line README/comment in the file pointing to the tool that consumes it; (b) if the wiring was meant to live here, add the small fetcher (mirror openbor-fetch-media.py) that downloads each url into downloaded_media/<system>/covers/<stem>.<ext>; (c) if it is truly dead, move it to _TMP. Also re-host the 3 archive.org/youtube-thumbnail URLs to stable copies.
confirmed.evidence: romhack-art-urls.json exists (5299 bytes, /home/deck/Emulation/tools/launchers/romhack-art-urls.json) but grep -rln 'romhack-art-urls' across both the launchers tree and /home/deck/esde-build/ES-DE returns no consumer. The only 'romhack' hits in launchers are an unrelated comment in wire-bezels.py:5 ('base-game match for translations/romhacks') and the JSON file itself. No code path loads it. Confirmed orphaned; low (dead data, no runtime effect).

confirmed.title: No baked default-positions file for the 'dualshock4' profile (every other key has one)
confirmed.description: The active gamepad-tester backend lib/madsrv/tester_cmds.py defines GP_PROFILES with key 'dualshock4' (0x054c:0x09cc 'DualShock 4', sprite dir dualshock4-tester) and _baked_positions(key) loads data/gp-defaults/gp-<key>-positions.json. A directory + sprite-stem cross-check shows EVERY other profile key has a matching, complete baked file whose keys equal its drawable sprite stems exactly (fc30=13/13, n30=18/18, dualsense=18/18, wiiupro=17/17, xbox360=16/16, steamdeck=24/24, wiimote=11/11, fc30ii=13/13 — identical to fc30 as intended, accessories nunchuk 3/3 and classic 17/17), but gp-dualshock4-positions.json is ABSENT even though art/icons/dualshock4-tester/ ships 17 sprites (circle, square, triangle, x, l1/l2/r1/r2, dpad*, lstick/rstick, ps, select, start). When a DualShock 4 is tested, _baked_positions('dualshock4') returns {} and gamepads.layout merges only the empty baked + any saved file, so the C++ page (GuiMadPageGamepads.cpp buildCanvasItems posOf returns false -> default grid 0.08+0.12*(index%8) / 0.15+0.25*(index/8)) lays every sprite out in a generic grid instead of on the controller photo.
confirmed.impact: Cosmetic only, and recoverable: a DualShock 4 tester opens with all button sprites in a meaningless grid over the photo until the user drag-aligns and Saves their own positions (which then persist in control-panel/gp-dualshock4-positions.json). No crash — the missing-file path is handled gracefully on both the Python (_read_json -> {}) and C++ (posOf->false) sides.
confirmed.fix: Add data/gp-defaults/gp-dualshock4-positions.json with the 17 stems positioned on the dualshock4-tester base art (mirror the gp-dualsense layout, which shares most stems — circle/square/triangle/x, l1/l2/r1/r2, dpad*, lstick/rstick, select/start; rename 'mic'/'ps' as appropriate: DS4 has 'ps' but no 'mic').
confirmed.evidence: Directory listing of data/gp-defaults/ contains positions files for classic, dualsense, fc30, fc30ii, n30, nunchuk, steamdeck, wiimote, wiiupro, xbox360 -- but NOT dualshock4. GP_PROFILES (mad_gamepad_tester.py:29-38) declares a 'dualshock4' profile (vid 0x054c pid 0x09cc), and a DS4 is a supported testable pad (_gp_pads skips only 0x16c0/0x28de, not 0x054c), so the profile is reachable when a DualShock 4 is connected. Every other GP_PROFILES key has a baked file; dualshock4 is the lone omission. Impact is graceful, not a crash: _gp_baked_positions (lines 460-473) returns {} when p.is_file() is False, and tester_cmds.py _baked_positions (233-235) returns {} for a missing file -- so the tester falls back to the auto grid layout (_gp_default_pos) instead of a controller-accurate layout until the user drags+saves. Confirmed missing file with cosmetic/data-completeness impact -> low.

confirmed.title: RapidJSON parses daemon lines with the default recursive descent and no depth limit on the reader thread
confirmed.description: parseLine() calls doc.Parse() with no flags. RAPIDJSON_PARSE_DEFAULT_FLAGS is kParseNoFlags (external/rapidjson/include/rapidjson/reader.h:140) and the default GenericReader is recursive, so deeply-nested JSON (e.g. thousands of nested arrays/objects) recurses proportionally to nesting depth. A pathological line from the daemon could exhaust the reader thread's stack and crash the process. The reader thread has a normal ~8MB stack so it takes substantial nesting to blow, and the daemon is our own trusted local Python script over a private pipe (no network), so reachability in normal use is essentially nil.
confirmed.impact: Theoretical DoS/crash only if the local daemon emits adversarial deeply-nested JSON; not reachable in normal operation. Noted for completeness given the IPC-robustness lens.
confirmed.fix: If hardening is desired, parse with kParseIterativeFlag (RapidJSON's non-recursive parser) to bound stack usage regardless of input nesting.
confirmed.evidence: MadJson.h parseLine (25-29) calls doc.Parse(line) with RapidJSON's default kParseDefaultFlags = recursive descent, no kParseIterativeFlag and no nesting depth limit. Deeply nested input could overflow the stack. This runs on the reader thread and the input is from the trusted local daemon, so it is a latent hardening note, not a reachable bug -> info.

confirmed.title: spawn() reassigns mReaderThread without asserting it is non-joinable — a latent std::terminate if a future path skips shutdownChild
confirmed.description: mReaderThread = std::thread{...} move-assigns onto the member. If mReaderThread were ever still joinable at that point, std::thread's move-assignment calls std::terminate(). Today every spawn() caller (initial in the panel ctor, restart()->terminate()->shutdownChild, handleChildDeath()->shutdownChild) joins the reader thread first (shutdownChild lines 479-486), so this is currently safe. It is flagged only as a fragility: a future refactor that calls spawn() without a preceding shutdownChild would hard-crash. Not a current bug.
confirmed.impact: No current crash path; latent maintenance hazard.
confirmed.fix: Add a defensive `if (mReaderThread.joinable()) mReaderThread.join();` (or an assert) at the top of spawn() so the invariant is self-documenting and can't regress silently.
confirmed.evidence: spawn() line 125 does mReaderThread = std::thread{...} with no prior assert/check that the existing thread is non-joinable; assigning to a joinable std::thread calls std::terminate. Verified no current path leaves it joinable: initial spawn() has a default-constructed (non-joinable) member; restart() calls terminate()->shutdownChild(true) which joins (479-486); handleChildDeath()->shutdownChild(false) joins before its spawn(). So today it is safe; it is a latent footgun for a future code path that reaches spawn() without going through shutdownChild -> info, exactly as titled.

confirmed.title: MadTheme::color() uses STOCK.at(key) which throws if a future MadColor enum value is added without updating the STOCK map
confirmed.description: color() falls back to `return STOCK.at(key);` as its last resort. STOCK currently contains all 11 MadColor enum values, so .at() never throws today. But std::map::at throws std::out_of_range on a missing key, and MadTheme::color() is called from render()/build() on the UI loop with no try/catch above it. If a maintainer adds a new MadColor enum entry and forgets to add it to STOCK (and the loaded theme + injected defaults also lack it), the next render throws an uncaught exception across the render loop. Latent footgun, not a current bug.
confirmed.impact: None today (map is exhaustive). Future-maintenance hazard: an uncaught exception in the render loop would terminate ES-DE.
confirmed.fix: Make the final fallback non-throwing (e.g. `auto it = STOCK.find(key); return it != STOCK.end() ? it->second : 0xFF00FFFF;`) or add a static_assert/comment tying the STOCK map to the enum so omissions are caught at build time.
confirmed.evidence: MadTheme::color (203) ends with `return STOCK.at(key);` which throws std::out_of_range if the key is absent. Verified the STOCK map (38-45) currently holds all 11 MadColor enumerators (Frame, Primary, Secondary, Title, Selector, Red, Green, Separators, PanelDimmed, ButtonFlatUnfocused, HelpText) matching the enum in MadTheme.h (28-40) exactly, so the throw is unreachable today. It is purely a latent maintenance hazard if a future enum value is added without updating STOCK; an uncaught exception would propagate up through the render/update loop. Matches the titled info severity -> info.

confirmed.title: Stale Daphne session scope can briefly show a wrong/failed map on panel reopen (self-heals)
confirmed.description: sScope/sGamedir/sBase are file-scope statics that persist across page AND panel instances (process-global). If the user selects a per-game scope, closes the panel, removes that Daphne game, then reopens the panel, build() calls load(sScope, sGamedir, sBase) with the stale per-game scope. The backend daphne.load for scope=="game" then fails.
confirmed.impact: No crash and no memory issue: the response handler (lines 60-67) detects scope=="game" failure, resets the statics to global, and reloads. Worst case is one extra round-trip and a momentary loading flash. This is deliberate Tk-parity session state, not a defect — flagged only for completeness.
confirmed.fix: None required. If desired, the statics could be reset to global in the GuiMadPageDaphne destructor or validated against the games list before the first load, but the existing fall-back path is correct.
confirmed.evidence: GuiMadPageDaphne.cpp:21-25 keeps file-scope statics sScope/sGamedir/sBase that persist across page instances; build() (line 38) seeds the first load with them. If a prior session left sScope=='game' with a now-removed gamedir, the load callback hits !ok and at lines 60-67 falls back to global and re-issues load('global','',''). All page members (mScope/mRows/mGames/mSections, lines 89-131) are per-instance and start empty; relayout() (line 77) only runs AFTER parse() populates them in the SUCCESS callback. Throughout, build() has already shown setLoadingText('Loading the Hypseus map…') (line 37), so no wrong/failed map is ever rendered — the user sees the loading text, then the global map (or an error flash). No crash, no memory issue; worst case is one extra backend round-trip on a stale per-game scope, fully masked by the loading text. Self-healing as the title claims; real-world impact is info, not low.

confirmed.title: GuiMadPageBackendDetail::mSuppressChildPopRefresh is set before the apply request; a never-arriving apply response leaves one stale frame (self-corrects)
confirmed.description: In the slot_profiles onChoose, mSuppressChildPopRefresh=true is set, then profiles.apply_slot (pageRequest, 10s timeout) is fired which is expected to refresh() in its response. onChildPopped (called when the choice page pops) consumes the flag and skips its own refresh. If the apply response never refreshes (e.g. a backend death between the pop and the response, dropping the pageRequest callback via the alive token), the detail page shows the pre-apply state until the next interaction.
confirmed.impact: Purely cosmetic/stale-display, not a memory or correctness hazard. The flag is consumed exactly once (set false in onChildPopped), so it cannot wedge future refreshes; the next child pop or section re-entry refreshes normally.
confirmed.fix: Acceptable as-is. Could be made fully robust by refreshing in onChildPopped unconditionally and letting the apply response refresh a second time, but that wastes a round-trip (which the current design intentionally avoids).
confirmed.evidence: GuiMadPageBackends.cpp:675 sets mSuppressChildPopRefresh=true BEFORE sending profiles.apply_slot; onChildPopped() (lines 349-357) then skips its refresh() once, deferring to the apply response. The apply response callback (lines 694-703) calls footer()->flash(...) and refresh() UNCONDITIONALLY — it does not gate on `ok` — and the request has a 10000ms timeout. MadBackend guarantees the callback always fires: on timeout it synthesizes ETIMEOUT and calls completeWithError(callback,'ETIMEOUT',...) (MadBackend.cpp:235-246), and on daemon death failAllPending() drives completeWithError for every pending request (MadBackend.cpp:575-590, callback(false,error)). MadPage::pageRequest only suppresses delivery if the page itself was destroyed (alive.expired(), MadPage.cpp:76). So refresh() always runs eventually. The only stale-frame window is up to ~10s while a still-alive-but-silent daemon never replies — a cosmetic stale slot label that self-corrects on timeout. Benign, info, exactly as titled.

confirmed.title: MadStepper auto-repeat divides (mMax-mMin)/mStep with no guard against mStep == 0
confirmed.description: adjust() computes `maxSteps = round((mMax - mMin) / mStep)` and `stepIndex = round((mValue - mMin) / mStep)`. A zero `mStep` would divide by zero (float div → inf/nan, then `static_cast<int>` of inf/nan is UB). I checked all four construction sites (GuiMadPageQuitCombo.cpp:209 step 0.1; GuiMadPageLightgun addStepper callers steps 0.01/0.1/50/1.0/20; GuiMadPageBackends.cpp:563 step 1.0 hardcoded). Every step is a hardcoded non-zero literal; none is derived from backend JSON. So the divisor is never zero in practice.
confirmed.impact: Not reachable with current callers. Purely defensive — flagged because a future backend-driven stepper range/step (the Backends page already feeds lo/hi from JSON, just not step) could introduce a 0 step.
confirmed.fix: In the MadStepper constructor clamp `mStep` to a tiny positive floor, e.g. `mStep {std::max(step, 1e-6f)}`, so adjust() can never divide by zero regardless of caller.
confirmed.evidence: MadStepper.cpp:90 computes maxSteps=round((mMax-mMin)/mStep) with no guard on mStep==0; for floats that yields inf (or nan if numerator is also 0), and the subsequent static_cast<int> of an out-of-float-range value (line 90/92) is UB. mStep is mMember from the ctor's `step` arg (MadStepper.cpp:33). Every caller supplies a nonzero step: Model2 number steppers default step to isFloat?0.1:1.0 (GuiMadPageModel2.cpp:171) and the backend table hardcodes step 0.1/1.0 (lib/madsrv/model2_cmds.py:60-71); enum/resolution steppers pass literal 1.0f (GuiMadPageModel2.cpp:151). So mStep==0 is only reachable if a malformed backend explicitly sends step:0, never in normal use. Theoretical only, exactly as titled; info severity is correct.

confirmed.title: Third-party release action pinned to a mutable major-version tag, not a commit SHA
confirmed.description: The publish step uses softprops/action-gh-release@v2 (a THIRD-PARTY action, not GitHub-maintained) pinned only to the floating major-version tag 'v2'. This job has 'permissions: contents: write' and passes the auto GITHUB_TOKEN, so a compromised/retagged v2 could in principle tamper with the published release assets. Supply-chain best practice for write-scoped third-party actions is to pin to a full commit SHA (e.g. softprops/action-gh-release@<40-char-sha>). actions/checkout@v4 (line 47) is GitHub-maintained, lower risk. Note also the recipe itself downloads appimagetool/linuxdeploy from 'continuous' (mutable, unpinned, no checksum) — but that is upstream-recipe code, called verbatim by design, not part of this workflow.
confirmed.impact: Low for a single-maintainer personal repo, but real: the workflow writes a release that the Steam Deck auto-pulls from /releases/latest, so a supply-chain compromise of the release action would land a tampered AppImage on the device.
confirmed.fix: Pin softprops/action-gh-release to a commit SHA with a trailing '# v2.x.y' comment, and consider pinning actions/checkout the same way. Optionally add Dependabot to bump the pinned SHAs.
confirmed.evidence: Factually true: line 101 `uses: softprops/action-gh-release@v2` pins a third-party action to a mutable major-version tag rather than a full commit SHA. OpenSSF/GitHub supply-chain hardening guidance does recommend SHA pinning for third-party actions, so the observation is valid. However real-world risk here is minimal: it's a single personal fork's release pipeline, the job token is scoped to contents: write only (lines 28-29), assets are integrity-checked on-device via the published ES-DE-MAD.AppImage.sha256 companion (line 93), and the first-party actions/checkout@v4 (line 47) is pinned the same major-tag way for consistency. This is a legitimate low/info hardening suggestion, not a bug on a normal path — confirmed as a true observation but downgraded to info severity. File: /home/deck/Emulation/tools/launchers/.github/workflows/build-appimage.yml:101.

refuted.title: MadSpriteCanvas indexes mItems[mSelection] guarded only by !empty(), not by mSelection < size()
refuted.description: nudgeSelected() (`Item& item {mItems[mSelection]};`) and render()'s selection-outline branch (`const Item& item {mItems[mSelection]};`) index the vector using only a `!mItems.empty()` guard. They never verify `mSelection < mItems.size()`. The other two accessors that touch mSelection are fully guarded: cycleSelection() takes it mod size (always valid) and selectedKey() explicitly checks `mSelection >= 0 && mSelection < size()`. clearItems() resets mSelection to 0. I traced every caller path (GuiMadPageXArcade, GuiMadPageGamepads): the canvas is always recreated fresh via make_shared on each rebuild, and item sets only grow via addItem or are wiped via clearItems (which resets mSelection). So there is no CURRENT path where mSelection outruns the item count while items remain non-empty.
refuted.impact: No reachable out-of-bounds today. It is a latent invariant: if a future caller ever removed items without going through clearItems() (e.g. an addItem/remove API), nudgeSelected and the render selection branch would read past the vector end (UAF/OOB on the render thread). Defensive only.
refuted.fix: Make the guard match the other accessors: in nudgeSelected and the render selection branch, replace `!mItems.empty()` with `mSelection >= 0 && mSelection < static_cast<int>(mItems.size())`, mirroring selectedKey().
refuted.evidence: MadSpriteCanvas.cpp nudgeSelected (169) and render (217-218) index mItems[mSelection] guarded only by !mItems.empty(), unlike selectedKey (158-163) which also checks 0<=mSelection<size(). But mSelection (init 0, MadSpriteCanvas.h:20) is ONLY mutated in two places: clearItems() resets it to 0 (line 76) and cycleSelection() always reassigns it mod mItems.size(), keeping it in [0,size) whenever non-empty (lines 154-155). Items are only ever appended via addItem()->emplace_back (line 69) — which grows the vector and can never make an existing in-range mSelection exceed the new size — or cleared wholesale via clearItems() which simultaneously resets mSelection to 0. There is no partial-removal path. Therefore mSelection is always a valid index whenever mItems is non-empty; the extra range checks in selectedKey() are belt-and-suspenders, not evidence of a reachable OOB. No live bug; refuted (defensive-inconsistency note at most, info).

refuted.title: MadChipRow::input passes a reference into mEntries to mOnToggle as the last statement, relying on no callback synchronously freeing the row
refuted.description: The 'a' handler binds `Entry& entry {mEntries[mCursor]};` then calls `mOnToggle(entry.chip.value, ...)`, passing `entry.chip.value` (a std::string living inside mEntries) to a `const std::string&` parameter. If a toggle callback synchronously rebuilt the page (freeing mEntries) it could read its own dangling reference mid-call. I audited every setOnToggle site (Backends, Daphne, Backup, Lightgun, Model2, Priority): all either call pageRequest() (async — copies value into the request lambda before returning) or do non-destructive in-place updates (Backup `mInclude[key]=on; updateTally()`; Model2 `setOption(...)` with deferred rollback). None destroys the chip row synchronously during the toggle, and input() does nothing with members after mOnToggle returns (it `return true`s immediately). MadTileGrid::input (mOnPick at :151) and MadReorderList follow the same safe last-statement-then-return shape.
refuted.impact: No bug today. Documented because the safety is by-convention (callbacks must not synchronously free the widget), not enforced — a future synchronous-rebuild toggle handler would turn this into a use-after-free of the const-ref argument.
refuted.fix: Optional hardening: copy the token before invoking, e.g. `const std::string value {entry.chip.value}; ... mOnToggle(value, ...);` so the argument lifetime is independent of mEntries.
refuted.evidence: MadChipRow::input (MadChipRow.cpp:129-138): mOnToggle(entry.chip.value, ...) is the LAST statement before `return true` and `entry` is never touched after the call, so even a synchronous row destruction inside the callback could only matter if the callback itself read the dangling `value&` AFTER destroying the row. It cannot, because the callbacks are asynchronous: every setOnToggle handler (GuiMadPageBackends.cpp:472, 512; Daphne 231; Lightgun 628; Model2 93; Backup 148) calls pageRequest -> MadBackend::request (MadBackend.cpp:387), which stashes the callback in mPending keyed by id and delivers it later from update() in the render loop, NOT synchronously. The ONLY synchronous path is completeWithError (MadBackend.cpp:393/410 -> 579-591) when mStdinFd==-1; the handlers it invokes (e.g. setBackendKey's error branch GuiMadPageBackends.cpp:386-396) call footer()->flash and refresh(), and refresh() (328-347) only enqueues another async request - it does NOT synchronously rebuild()/destroy mEntries. Handlers that DO read the value reference synchronously (Lightgun presets 628-641 via sscanf) update OTHER widgets and never free their own row. The 'Optimistic; the page reverts on failure' comment shows the design is intentional and safe. No current caller violates the pattern; this is a latent style note, not a bug.

refuted.title: AppImage recipe has no 'set -e'; CI relies solely on the post-build test -f guard
refuted.description: The build step runs 'bash tools/create_AppImage_SteamDeck.sh' then 'test -f ES-DE_x64_SteamDeck.AppImage || exit 1'. The recipe (tools/create_AppImage_SteamDeck.sh, upstream, called verbatim) uses '#!/usr/bin/bash' with NO 'set -e' and bare 'exit' (status 0) on its own failure gates (.clang-format check line 23, SDL-PipeWire check line 56). So an intermediate failure (e.g. make, or the linuxdeploy/appimagetool bundle step) does not abort the recipe; it would proceed to the final mv/echo and exit 0. The CI's explicit 'test -f' artifact assertion is what actually fails the job, and the comment at lines 78-80 documents this on purpose. On a fresh GitHub runner the workspace is clean so there is no stale AppImage to make the guard pass falsely; the guard is therefore sound in practice.
refuted.impact: Low/none on fresh CI runners (each run is an isolated workspace, so no stale artifact can satisfy test -f after a failed build). The residual risk is only that a build failure produces a confusing late/cryptic failure rather than failing at the offending step, which the comment already acknowledges.
refuted.fix: Optional hardening: the workflow could additionally assert the artifact is non-empty and freshly produced (e.g. check file size > some threshold, or 'find -newermt' since step start). This is upstream-recipe behaviour, not our patch, so changing the recipe is out of scope; the CI guard is acceptable as-is.
refuted.evidence: Premise is wrong: the workflow has no shell:/defaults: override (grep confirmed), so every run: block uses GitHub Actions' default Linux shell `bash --noprofile --norc -e -o pipefail {0}` — errexit AND pipefail are already on at the step level. Therefore the step does NOT rely 'solely' on the test -f guard: if `bash tools/create_AppImage_SteamDeck.sh` (line 83) returns non-zero, the step's own -e fails the step. The `test -f ES-DE_x64_SteamDeck.AppImage || { ...; exit 1; }` at lines 84-85 is a deliberate, documented backstop (comment lines 78-80) for the recipe's KNOWN exit-0 abort behaviour ('failure gates use bare exit (status 0)'), which is the only failure mode -e cannot catch. Both failure paths fail the step. Whether the inner shell script uses set -e is the inner script's concern, not this YAML's. The two-layer guard is correct and intentional, not a defect. File: /home/deck/Emulation/tools/launchers/.github/workflows/build-appimage.yml:81-85.

criticFindings.title: MadWiiBridge claims a PR_SET_PDEATHSIG backstop that does not exist — EOF-on-stdin is the only lifeline
criticFindings.line: 68-82 (child fork, no prctl); comments at 88 and 122
criticFindings.description: The forked child (lines 68-82) execs python3 wii-nav-bridge.py without ever calling prctl(PR_SET_PDEATHSIG, SIGTERM). Yet two comments assert PDEATHSIG is the bridge's safety net: line 88 'PDEATHSIG would be its only lifeline' and line 122 'EOF ... PDEATHSIG is the backup.' The ONLY actual termination mechanism is the daemon noticing EOF when ES-DE closes sStdinFd (shutdown(), or process exit). There is no kernel-level parent-death signal. The CLOEXEC on sStdinFd (line 89) correctly stops a launched game from inheriting the write end, so EOF normally arrives — but if anything keeps the pipe open or the Python side fails to exit on EOF, the bridge daemon leaks for the whole login session with no backstop, contrary to the code's own stated design.
criticFindings.impact: Orphaned wii-nav-bridge.py process surviving ES-DE exit in the (uncommon) case EOF is not seen; the documented 'PDEATHSIG backup' that a reader would rely on is not implemented. No crash. Trivially fixable by adding prctl(PR_SET_PDEATHSIG, SIGTERM) in the child right after fork (Linux-only, which is the only target).
criticFindings.fix: In the child branch (after fork, before execlp) add: #include <sys/prctl.h>; prctl(PR_SET_PDEATHSIG, SIGTERM); — then the comments become true and EOF is genuinely a secondary path. Alternatively, correct the comments to say the sole mechanism is stdin EOF.

criticFindings.title: GuiMadCaptureModal::render() assumes mPanel is the gui directly beneath it on the Window stack
criticFindings.line: 242-251 (render); pushed from e.g. GuiMadPagePlayers.cpp:128, GuiMadPageQuitCombo.cpp:322/724, GuiMadPagePreview.cpp:278
criticFindings.description: The modal compensates for Window only rendering bottom+top gui by manually drawing mPanel first (mPanel->render(parentTrans)) then itself, to repaint the opaque panel that would otherwise be skipped. This is correct ONLY while the capture modal is the immediate top gui and the panel is immediately beneath. mPanel is a raw pointer captured at construction (mPanel{panel}); it stays valid because the panel cannot be popped while the modal sits above it. But if any other Gui is pushed on top of the capture modal (e.g. a GuiMsgBox error from some other subsystem, or a future code path), Window would render that new top gui + the bottom-most gui, and the capture modal's own render (which double-renders the panel) would not run at all or would run at the wrong stacking level. Today no path pushes over the modal (its input() returns true and swallows everything, and it self-finishes), so it is not currently reachable — flagged as a fragile invariant rather than a live bug.
criticFindings.impact: No current crash or visual bug. A future change that pushes any Gui above an open capture modal would produce a missing/incorrect backdrop. Latent coupling to Window's two-layer render assumption.
criticFindings.fix: Document the invariant at the render() override, or have the modal render unconditionally opaque (fill its own background fully) so it does not depend on re-rendering mPanel; long term, mark the modal so Window renders the full stack beneath it.

criticFindings.title: Splash uses a raw SwitchComponent* in an async response while every sibling page that learned the lesson uses weak_ptr — confirms W4.0 is a real inconsistency, not a pattern
criticFindings.line: 181 (raw sc), 193-203 (async deref); contrast GuiMadPageBackends.cpp:510 weakChips, GuiMadPageSystems.cpp:282-287 lookup-by-key
criticFindings.description: This corroborates and sharpens the existing W4.0 finding. The codebase has THREE switch/chip-toggle pages whose response callbacks may outlive the widget across a rebuild: (a) GuiMadPageBackends.cpp:510 uses std::weak_ptr<MadChipRow> with an explicit comment 'a raw pointer would dangle'; (b) GuiMadPageSystems.cpp:282-287 re-looks-up the SwitchComponent by flag/id from mToggles in the response (never derefs a captured raw pointer); (c) GuiMadPageSplash.cpp:181/193-203 captures a RAW SwitchComponent* and derefs it (sc->setState) in both the error and success branches of the async splash.toggle_image response. Splash is the lone outlier. I verified applyConfig() (line 73-86) does NOT itself rebuild the list, so the dereference at line 203 is safe within a single callback; the bug is strictly the cross-action race the original finding described (a MODE/FIT cycle calls rebuildList() and frees the switch list while a pool-toggle response is in flight). Because the same author used weak_ptr correctly two files over, this is an oversight, and the fix is mechanical.
criticFindings.impact: Use-after-free / heap-write-after-free on a SwitchComponent if the user toggles a splash-pool entry and then changes MODE or FIT (which rebuilds the list) before the toggle response returns. Crash or memory corruption, reachable in normal use on the random-image splash page.
criticFindings.fix: Mirror Backends: capture std::weak_ptr<SwitchComponent> weakSc {switchComp}; in the response, auto sc{weakSc.lock()}; if(!sc) return; before setState. Or mirror Systems: re-find the live switch by name in mRows/the list.

criticFindings.title: ApplicationUpdater non-numeric guard still lets std::stoi throw std::out_of_range on an oversized all-digit release number (corroborates W6.2; confirmed live on the updater thread)
criticFindings.description: Confirming and adding precision to W6.2. The new guard at 417-419 rejects any release number containing a non-digit and 'continue's, but std::stoi(releaseType->releaseNum) at line 425 still throws std::out_of_range for an all-digit string larger than INT_MAX (e.g. '99999999999999'). compareVersions() runs on the updater's detached thread (ApplicationUpdater::update spawns mThread); an uncaught std::out_of_range there calls std::terminate and aborts ES-DE at startup. The feed is CI-generated from github.run_number so this is not adversarially reachable today, but the guard was clearly intended to make a malformed feed safe and it does not fully do so. The MAD_RELEASE_NUMBER macro side (CMake add_compile_definitions from $ENV) has the same class of risk but is build-time only.
criticFindings.impact: Startup abort if the published latest_release.json ever carries a release number above 2^31-1 (run numbers won't reach that, but a hand-edited or corrupted feed would). Defeats the stated goal of the guard ('a malformed releaseNum must not throw on the updater thread').
criticFindings.fix: Replace std::stoi with std::strtol/std::from_chars into a long with range handling, or wrap the comparison in try/catch and 'continue' on any exception. Same treatment for the Android androidVersionCode stoi if reachable.

criticFindings.title: MadTileGrid::render does not guard a degenerate (rounds-to-zero) clip rect, unlike its sibling MadScrollView
