criticFindings.description: MadScrollView::render (MadScrollView.cpp:77-82) explicitly checks 'if (clipDim.x < 1 || clipDim.y < 1) return;' with a comment that pushClipRect treats a zero dimension as 'extend to the screen edge', which would DISABLE clipping. MadTileGrid::render computes the same scale-aware dims at 213-214 and calls pushClipRect at 215-218 with NO such guard. If the grid is ever laid out at a sub-pixel size (dim rounds to 0), clipping is silently disabled and offscreen tile rows would paint outside the viewport. Not a crash (popClipRect still balances; render early-returns on mEntries.empty()), and grids are always given a real viewport, so not currently reachable — purely a consistency gap with the documented sibling.
criticFindings.impact: Cosmetic only and not currently reachable; tiles could bleed outside a zero-height grid. Worth matching MadScrollView's guard for robustness.
criticFindings.fix: Add the same clipDim < 1 early-return before pushClipRect, matching MadScrollView.

criticFindings.title: GamescopeFocus::debugLog reopens the log file on every focus transition with no error handling on a power-loss-prone path
criticFindings.description: debugLog opens an std::ofstream in append mode and closes it on every call. It is invoked on each focus gained/lost transition (hasFocus lines 174-178) and at init. This is on the render thread. The open/append/close per event is cheap relative to a frame and only fires on transitions (not per-frame), and it bails if HOME is unset, so this is fine — flagged only as a minor inefficiency / unbounded log growth (the file under ~/Emulation/storage/controller-router/gamescope-focus.log is never rotated). Combined with the existing W7.2 (no XSetIOErrorHandler), the X11 path is the riskier part; this logging is benign.
criticFindings.impact: Negligible. Log file grows without bound across many focus transitions over a long session; no functional impact.
criticFindings.fix: Optional: cap/rotate the log, or open once and keep the stream. Not required.

criticFindings.title: Build packaging is correct: all 62 MAD sources/headers are in es-app/CMakeLists.txt, X11 is linked for GamescopeFocus, and MAD_RELEASE_NUMBER is wired CMake<->CI<->updater consistently
criticFindings.description: Positive confirmation, not a defect. I cross-checked every new .cpp/.h against the diff --name-only list: all 30 MAD .cpp and 31 MAD .h files plus GamescopeFocus.cpp/.h are listed in ES_SOURCES/ES_HEADERS. X11 is found+linked only in the Linux else() branch that builds the es-de target (find_package(X11 REQUIRED); target_link_libraries(es-de ... X11_LIBRARIES)), which is exactly where GamescopeFocus.cpp (guarded by #if defined(__linux__) && !defined(__ANDROID__)) needs it. MAD_RELEASE_NUMBER flows: CMakeLists.txt reads $ENV{MAD_RELEASE_NUMBER} -> add_compile_definitions; the workflow sets it to github.run_number for the build step; ApplicationUpdater compares the feed's 'release' (also github.run_number) against the baked macro, defaulting to 0 for local builds. The two run_number sources match, so the updater never false-positives on a CI build of the same run. The workflow asserts the AppImage artifact exists (recipe failure gates exit 0), pins ubuntu-22.04 for glibc compat, and uses concurrency cancel-in-progress:false to avoid truncated release uploads. The only build-file nit already filed is W8.3 (softprops/action-gh-release@v2 pinned to a mutable tag).
criticFindings.impact: None — documents that the build/link/bundle wiring is sound and complete, so a broken-link or missing-source class of bug is ruled out.
criticFindings.fix: No change needed. (Optional hardening already captured by W8.3.)

criticFindings.title: FileData game-end event param count (producer) matches ViewController consumer at the new size 6 — the 5th scripting arg patch is internally consistent
criticFindings.line: FileData.cpp:2256-2264 (producer, 6 emplace_back); views/ViewController.cpp:1265-1269 (consumer, size()==6, arg index [5]); Scripting.cpp/h (5-arg fireEvent)
criticFindings.description: Positive confirmation. The patch adds a 5th script argument (launched-from custom collection) to game-start/game-end. I verified the deferred-fire path: FileData::launchGame for runInBackground pushes exactly 6 strings into gameEndEventParams (romPath, name, system, fullName, collection), and ViewController::input now checks 'if (eventParams.size() == 6)' and passes eventParams[5]. Scripting::fireEvent's signature gained arg5 with a default, and the quoting/append logic for arg5 mirrors arg1-4. No off-by-one or stale size==5 check remains in the consumer. (Note: the producer comment says 'arg5: launched-from custom collection' but emplaces 5 data fields after romPath = 5 args total beyond the event name; the count is correct, the 6 refers to the vector length including romPath.)
criticFindings.impact: None — rules out a vector-size mismatch / out-of-bounds on the game-end scripting path that the size constant change could have introduced.

criticFindings.title: PDFViewer standalone (null-FileData) path is fully null-safe — no residual mGame-> deref reachable from the new path/title overload
criticFindings.line: 78-82 (guarded mGame deref), 211-218 (new overload), 229-234 (launchMediaViewer guard), 723-728 (getHelpPrompts guard), input->776 launchMediaViewer
criticFindings.description: Positive confirmation of the new Window::PdfHandler pure-virtual overload startPDFViewer(path,title) (added to the abstract interface in Window.h, implemented only by PDFViewer, so no missing-override compile break). I grepped every mGame use: line 80-81 deref is now guarded by 'if (game != nullptr)'; the rest of startPDFViewer(FileData*) after line 82 uses mManualPath (preset by the overload) and never touches mGame again; launchMediaViewer() returns early when mGame==nullptr (line 231) and is the only thing the 'down' input action (line 776) calls; getHelpPrompts() guards the 'game media' prompt (line 726). So opening a standalone user-manual PDF from GuiMenu's USER MANUALS row cannot null-deref. The caller (GuiMenu.cpp:2395) sources the path from a directory scan of ~/ES-DE/usermanuals (trusted local files).
criticFindings.impact: None — rules out a null-pointer crash on the new PDF feature, which was the obvious risk when a path-based open reuses the game-based code with a null FileData.

finderNotes.notes:
SCOPE: Read in full MadBackend.{h,cpp}, MadJson.h, MadWiiBridge.{h,cpp}; cross-read GuiMadCaptureModal.cpp, GuiMadPanel.cpp (poll/update/request sites), MadPage.cpp (pageRequest), and grepped every pages/*.cpp request call site.

=== IPC SERIALIZATION CONCLUSION (the key Python wave-1/2 cross-check) ===
The C++ client is NOT a synchronous request/response handshake. MadBackend::request() (MadBackend.cpp:387-412) is fire-and-forget: it assigns an incrementing id, stores a PendingRequest (only if callback != null), writes ONE NDJSON line to the pipe via writeLine(), and returns immediately. It does NOT block waiting for the response. Responses arrive asynchronously on the dedicated reader thread (readerLoop) which pushes parsed Documents onto the mutex-guarded mQueue; the UI thread drains them once per frame in poll()->dispatchMessage(). Therefore MULTIPLE REQUESTS CAN BE OUTSTANDING AT ONCE — pages routinely issue several requests back-to-back without awaiting (e.g. GuiMadPanel issues art.resolve + devices.watch + requestSidebarIcons; pages issue policy.merged then systems.list, etc.).

HOWEVER, the property the Python refutations actually need still holds: (1) ALL request() calls originate on the SINGLE UI thread — the only callers are page build/input/update and poll-driven response/event/stream callbacks, plus GuiMadCaptureModal::update() which calls the SAME backend's poll() (UI thread). I found NO request() invocation from the reader thread or any other thread. (2) All requests go out over ONE shared blocking fd (mStdinFd) in monotonically-increasing id order via writeLine(), which fully writes each line before returning. So from the daemon's perspective the C++ side is a STRICTLY ORDERED, SINGLE-WRITER stream of requests on one stdin pipe, delivered one NDJSON line at a time in id order.

Implication for the Python findings: the daemon receives requests serialized on its single stdin reader (one line at a time, in order) from exactly one client. So the wave-2 refutations of [0.2] unlocked _ENUM_CACHE, [2.1] unlocked set_ra_option RMW, and [10.2] hotplug watcher are CORRECT *to the extent* they only required "requests arrive serially on one stdin thread and there is a single client." The C++ side confirms single-client + serialized-on-the-wire + single-writer. 

CAVEAT for the Python side to be aware of: "serialized on the wire / dispatched in order off stdin" is NOT the same as "handlers complete before the next request is dispatched." The C++ client will happily PIPELINE — send request N+1 before the response to request N arrives. If mad-backend.py reads its stdin on one thread but hands SLOW methods to a ThreadPool (as the prompt states), then two slow methods, or a slow method concurrent with a fast method, CAN execute concurrently on the Python side even though the C++ client is single-threaded — because the C++ side does not wait. So any Python finding that was refuted specifically on "the C++ client blocks for each response so two handlers never overlap" is NOT safe — the C++ client does not block. Findings refuted on "single stdin reader thread dispatches requests serially" remain safe ONLY for state mutated synchronously on that stdin-reader thread (not for state mutated inside ThreadPool worker callbacks). The C++ side can issue overlapping slow calls; whether they actually overlap depends entirely on the Python dispatch/threadpool design, which is out of my scope but should be double-checked against any RMW done in a worker thread.

=== LIFETIME / THREAD-SAFETY VERDICT (mostly clean) ===
- Cross-thread shared state: only mQueue is touched by the reader thread (under mQueueMutex); mDead/mReaderDone are atomic. mPending, mStreamCallbacks, mEventCallbacks, mUnclaimedStreams, mNextId, mRestartTimes are UI-thread-only. Confirmed readerLoop only appends to mQueue and sets the two atomics. No data race found.
- Iterator-invalidation seams: poll() drains mQueue into a local vector before dispatching (good); dispatchMessage copies the callback out before erasing mPending and before invoking (lines 275/285/287, good); the ETIMEOUT loop (236-246) copies callback, advances/erases the iterator, THEN calls completeWithError — a re-entrant request() only inserts a NEW map node (no invalidation of the advanced iterator) and a re-entrant erase of a DIFFERENT node also doesn't invalidate it (std::map erase only invalidates the erased iterator). Safe. closeAllStreams() collect-then-clear-then-invoke is correct; a callback's dtor calling clearStreamCallback on the already-cleared map is a harmless no-op erase. deliverUnclaimedStreams moves the batch out and erases before dispatching. All correct.
- fd hygiene: spawn() closes both unused pipe ends in parent (115-116) and closes all four on fork/pipe failure; CLOEXEC on all pipes so launched games don't inherit them; shutdownChild closes mStdinFd then mStdoutFd and nulls them. No fd leak found.
- mReaderThread is always joined in shutdownChild before any spawn() reassignment (see finding re: latent fragility, currently safe).
- SIGPIPE save/restore in spawn()/dtor is correct (captured once on first spawn so a restart can't capture our own SIG_IGN). MadWiiBridge uses signal(SIGPIPE, SIG_IGN) process-globally and never restores it, but that is per the comment intentional and out of the MadBackend scope; note it sets SIG_IGN that games may inherit (MadWiiBridge.cpp:51) — lower concern than MadBackend which carefully restores.
- mNextId never resets across restarts (no stale-id collision after respawn) — good.
- shutdownChild on the crash path (handleChildDeath, UI thread) can block up to ~2s reaping (40x50ms) + up to ~2s joining the reader (40x50ms) = ~4s of UI-thread stall with no rendering. On the clean teardown path the daemon exits promptly on stdin EOF so the loops exit early. I considered flagging this but it matches the prompt's stated/accepted design ("confirm the ~4s blocking reap is acceptable vs a visible render stall"); it is a known, bounded, rare (crash-only) stall, so I left it as a note rather than a finding. Worth a one-line acknowledgment to the user that a backend crash can briefly freeze the panel.

=== JSON HELPER BOUNDS (MadJson.h) — clean ===
- getMember returns a shared static null sentinel for non-objects/missing keys, so chained lookups never deref null. getString/getBool/getInt all type-check and fall back to a default; getString uses GetString()+GetStringLength() (binary-safe, no strlen overrun). No stoul/stoi/at() anywhere in the IPC path (only one substr in readerLoop with a bounds-correct npos-guarded index). getInt's GetDouble()->int cast for out-of-int-range numbers is implementation-defined but cannot crash and merely fails to match a pending id (harmless). No buffer/bounds bug found in MadJson.h.
- makeRequest builds the line with RapidJSON's Writer (proper escaping); returns std::string(buffer.GetString(), buffer.GetSize()) (binary-safe). No injection risk.

Unbounded mQueue for responses (never capped, only events are): a flood of response-shaped lines could grow mQueue between frames, but poll() fully drains every frame and unmatched ids are dropped in dispatch; not a realistic issue, did not flag.

IPC SERIALIZATION / THREADING CONCLUSION (requested): The reader thread (MadBackend::readerLoop) touches ONLY: a local `buffer`, mQueue via enqueue() under mQueueMutex, and the two std::atomic flags mDead/mReaderDone. All other state (mPending, mStreamCallbacks, mEventCallbacks, mUnclaimedStreams, mNextId, mState, mRestartTimes) is read/written exclusively on the UI thread (poll/request/dispatchMessage/shutdownChild). I found NO cross-thread access to those structures, so the single mutex on mQueue + the two atomics is sufficient — no data race. The poll() ETIMEOUT loop erases from mPending then calls completeWithError on a COPIED callback (line 238-241); std::map insertion (if the callback re-enters request()) does not invalidate the surviving iterator, so that loop is safe. dispatchMessage copies stream/event callbacks before invoking (lines 341/366), and closeAllStreams collects+clears the map before invoking (515-535) so a callback that deletes its owner and calls clearStreamCallback hits an already-cleared map (harmless no-op). NDJSON framing in readerLoop handles partial reads and discards unparseable lines. This all matches the recon and I found no defect in the IPC layer within my scope.

THREADING / CONCURRENCY CONCLUSION: GamescopeFocus has NO threads of its own. The global `gamescopeFocus` object (main.cpp:74), all Xlib calls (XOpenDisplay/XGetWindowProperty/XInternAtom), and the focus-transition video pause/resume are all touched exclusively on the UI/main thread inside applicationLoop(). There is no cross-thread shared state and no IPC serialization concern in this component (the only pipe IPC here, MadWiiBridge, is fire-and-forget single-line writes from the UI thread; no reader thread, no JSON framing). synthesis.risk_summary: The C++ MAD patches are, on the whole, well-engineered: the IPC layer (MadBackend/MadJson) is carefully written with correct fd hygiene, SIGPIPE save/restore, atomic cross-thread flags, mutex-guarded queue, and bounds-safe JSON accessors — no data race, no iterator-invalidation, no buffer overrun was found there. Risk concentrates in TWO confirmed memory-safety defects on common paths, both verified against source: (1) W7.0/W6.0 — the new per-frame Steam-Deck focus handler in es-app/src/main.cpp:549-562 calls ViewController::getInstance()->startViewVideos()/pauseViewVideos() on the first focus transition; those inline overrides at es-app/src/views/ViewController.h:87/:89 dereference mCurrentView with NO null guard (it is initialized to nullptr at ViewController.cpp:47 and stays null in the no-ROMs / invalid-systems startup state, while sibling methods DO guard it) → null-pointer crash for any user who opens the Steam overlay/QAM while ES-DE is sitting on an empty/error gamelist. (2) W4.0 — GuiMadPageSplash captures a RAW SwitchComponent* into an async backend response lambda (GuiMadPageSplash.cpp:181-209) that a concurrent MODE/FIT rebuild frees via mList.reset() (rebuildList, lines 88-97/320) → use-after-free if a splash response lands after the user changed mode. Both are "high" rather than "critical" because each needs a specific (but realistic) timing/state: W7.0 needs the empty-gamelist state plus an overlay toggle; W4.0 needs a mode switch racing an in-flight toggle response. Everything else is low/info robustness hardening. No critical, no corruption-of-user-data finding. Net: ship-blocking only on the two high memory-safety fixes; the rest are polish.
themes: MEMORY-SAFETY is the dominant theme and it is a CONSISTENCY problem, not a systemic one: nearly every async page (GuiMadPageBackends weakChips at :510, GuiMadPageSystems lookup-by-key at :282-287) correctly uses weak_ptr or re-lookup-by-key to survive a rebuild between request and response. GuiMadPageSplash is the lone holdout that captures a raw widget pointer (W4.0/CC.2) — a real inconsistency, not the prevailing pattern.

NULL-DEREF FROM NEW UPSTREAM INTEGRATION: the highest-severity bug (W7.0/W6.0) is not in the MAD panel at all but in the small native PauseGames/focus glue bolted into the upstream render loop (main.cpp) calling into upstream ViewController without matching upstream's own null-guard discipline.

IPC ROBUSTNESS / DAEMON-DEATH degradation: a cluster of low findings (W0.0 unbounded line buffer, W0.1 blocking stdin write on the UI thread, W1.0 missed input.lock watchdog, W1.1 ~2-4s blocking reap on crash/RETRY/close) all share one root: the backend pipe work runs on the UI/render thread, so a misbehaving or dying daemon degrades to a frame stall or a wedged panel rather than graceful recovery. Bounded and rare, but all the same shape.

UNGUARDED PARSE / ARITHMETIC EDGE CASES (defensive-coding, mostly info): unguarded GetString() on a Wii 'allowed' element (W2.0), std::clamp with possibly-inverted bounds (W4.1), std::stoi out_of_range on an oversized all-digit version (W6.2/CC.3), STOCK.at() / clamp-by-zero-step / no rapidjson depth limit (W1.2,W5.1,W0.2). None reachable from normal config today; they bite only on malformed/hostile/future backend output.

PROCESS / SIGNAL ENVIRONMENT hygiene (low): MadWiiBridge sets SIGPIPE to SIG_IGN process-globally and never restores it (W6.1), and its claimed PR_SET_PDEATHSIG backstop does not actually exist (CC.0) — EOF-on-stdin is the only child lifeline. GamescopeFocus has no XSetIOErrorHandler so a broken X connection kills ES-DE via Xlib's default fatal handler (W7.2).

LAUNCHERS CONFIG GAPS (low, non-C++): romhack-art-urls.json is orphaned with no consumer (W8.0); the 'dualshock4' gp-defaults profile has no baked default-positions file though every other profile does (W8.1).

synthesis.seam_conclusion: VERIFIED CONCURRENCY/SERIALIZATION ANSWER (read MadBackend.{h,cpp}, MadJson.h, MadWiiBridge, plus every pages/*.cpp request call site): The C++ client is single-client, single-writer, and strictly ordered ON THE WIRE, but it is NOT a synchronous handshake — it PIPELINES. MadBackend::request() (MadBackend.cpp:387-412) is fire-and-forget: assign incrementing id, store PendingRequest only if a callback exists, writeLine() one NDJSON line over the single blocking mStdinFd, return immediately. Responses arrive on a dedicated reader thread that only appends to a mutex-guarded mQueue; the UI thread drains it once per frame in poll()->dispatchMessage(). ALL request() calls originate on the SINGLE UI thread (page build/input/update, poll-driven response/event/stream callbacks, and GuiMadCaptureModal::update() which polls the SAME backend) — NO request() call from the reader thread or any other thread. So the daemon sees exactly one client, one stdin reader, lines in monotonic id order, one writer. CONFIRMS DEAD: Python [0.2] (unlocked _ENUM_CACHE) and [10.2]/[10.x] (hotplug watcher) — both were refuted on 'requests arrive serially on one stdin thread + single client,' which the C++ side fully confirms, AND they concern state mutated synchronously on that stdin-reader thread, so they stay dead. CONDITIONALLY REVIVES / MUST RE-CHECK: Python [2.1] (set_ra_option read-modify-write). It stays dead ONLY IF the RMW executes synchronously on the Python stdin-reader thread. If mad-backend.py dispatches that handler (or any slow handler) to a ThreadPool, the C++ client's pipelining means request N+1 is on the wire before N's response returns, so two handlers CAN overlap on the Python side — the C++ client does NOT block. Therefore any Python refutation justified by 'the C++ client blocks per response so handlers never overlap' is INVALID and must be re-judged against the Python threadpool/dispatch design. Net: serialization-on-the-wire is confirmed; mutual-exclusion-of-handler-execution is NOT, and is the one place the Python-side RMW verdict needs a second look.
synthesis.coverage_gaps: STATIC-ONLY — no build/run was performed (per scope). (2) W7.0/W6.0 crash repro: needs an on-device run in the actual no-ROMs / invalid-systems empty-gamelist state, then toggle the Steam overlay/QAM, to confirm the null-deref fires (and that the fix prevents it) — cannot be observed headlessly (no display). (3) W4.0 use-after-free: needs ASAN or a timing repro (open Splash page, toggle a pool image, switch MODE/FIT before the response lands) — static analysis confirms the dangling capture but not the exact race window; an ASAN build would settle it. (4) W1.0 / W1.1 / W0.1 UI-thread stalls: the ~2-4s reap, missed-input.lock wedge, and blocking-write frame stall are bounded by reasoning but their real-world visibility (does the panel visibly freeze? does input truly wedge?) needs an on-device test with the daemon killed mid-request. (5) The Python-side [2.1] RMW concurrency question above is OUT OF THIS WAVE'S SCOPE — it requires reading mad-backend.py's dispatch/threadpool to decide whether pipelined overlapping calls actually hit the set_ra_option read-modify-write. (6) MadWiiBridge child-lifetime (CC.0): the claimed PR_SET_PDEATHSIG backstop is absent; whether the Wii bridge child is correctly reaped on an ES-DE crash (vs. EOF-on-stdin being the only lifeline) should be checked live. (7) The launchers gaps (W8.0 orphaned JSON, W8.1 missing dualshock4 defaults) were reproduced by execution but the user-visible impact (does dualshock4 fall back to a sane default at runtime?) needs an on-device profile test.
